revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Temple Fujii de Fujiidera]][[de:Fujii-dera (Fujiidera)]][[en:Fujii-dera]][[fr:Fujii-dera]][[ja:葛井寺]] {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Japan}} {{for|the site in Yoshinogawa|Fujii-dera (Yoshinogawa)}} [[File:Fujiidera hondo1024.jpg|thumb|Main Hall of Fujii-dera]]
{{nihongo|Fujii-dera|葛井寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} in [[:en:Fujiidera, Osaka|Fujiidera, Osaka]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The temple is associated with {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}} and has as its main image a sculpture of the {{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|en|Thousand-armed Kannon|qid=Q10904910|lt=Thousand-armed Kannon|1=Thousand-armed Kannon}}. It is the fifth temple on the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qid=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}.
== Overview == The temple was founded on the order of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|qid=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} in 725 and consecrated by the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|qid=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}}. It became part of a system of provincial temples (''[[:en:Kokubun-ji|Kokubun-ji]]'') founded by the state with the purpose of providing prayers and other services for the protection of the nation and the Imperial House. Archaeological finds on the temple grounds confirm the foundation of the temple in the 8th century and the connection to the Fujii family, descendants of the royal house of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]], which had migrated to Japan.
The temple was promoted by the imperial family through the centuries. Renovations are known by {{ill|Prince Abo|en|Prince Abo|qid=Q11657231|lt=Prince Abo|1=Prince Abo}} in 806 and by {{ill|Ariwara no Narihira|en|Ariwara no Narihira|qid=Q451991|lt=Ariwara no Narihira|1=Ariwara no Narihira}}. Among the patrons of the temple was the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} politician {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}. In 1096, [[Fujii Yasumoto]] had a number of buildings restored. The Emperors [[:en:Go-Daigo|Go-Daigo]] and [[:en:Go-Murakami|Go-Murakami]] are said to have been admirers of the temple's main image. The temple was destroyed by earthquake in 1510 and renovated in 1602 by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|qid=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori|1=Toyotomi Hideyori}}. The four gates of the temple date from this time. The present {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|qid=Q17051413|lt=main hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}} was completed in 1776.
The main image is a seated sculpture of a thousand-armed and eleven-headed Kannon, produced in a dry lacquer technology, and designated as a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}. The statue has a total of 1041 arms: 2 main arms with the hand palms facing each other in front of the statue, 38 large and 1001 small arms extending from behind the body.<ref>{{Harvnb|Ogawa|Seki|Yamazaki|2009|pp=676–677}}</ref><ref name="ACA">{{cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title = ja:国指定文化財 データベース | publisher = Database of National Cultural Properties | access-date = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = Japanese | archive-date = 2009-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20090330140022/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | url-status = dead }}</ref>
== See also == *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|qid=Q3245521|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)}}
== References == {{Reflist}} * Patricia Frame Rugola: ''The Saikoku Kannon Pilgrimage Route.'' Dissertation, Ohio State University, 1986. * Valeria Jana Schwanitz und August Wierling: ''Saigoku - Unterwegs in Japans westlichen Landen.'' Manpuku-Verlag, Potsdam 2012, {{ISBN|978-3-9815168-0-7}}. {{refbegin}} *{{Cite book | edition = 2nd | publisher = YAMA-KEI | isbn = 978-4-635-09031-5 | last1 = Ogawa | first1 = Kouzou | last2 = Seki | first2 = Nobuko | last3 = Yamazaki | first3 = Takayuki | script-title=ja:仏像 | location = Tokyo | series = 山溪カラー名鑑 | year = 2009 | language = Japanese }}. {{refend}} {{Coord |34.570186|N|135.596556|E|type:landmark_dim:100_region:JP-30|display=title}}
[[en:Fujii-dera]] {{wikidata link|Q6406788}} [[Category:725 establishments]] [[Category:8th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist pilgrimage sites in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 720s]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Temples of Avalokiteśvara]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
